perturbation.explanation: The original text contains clear obligations regarding confidentiality. The modified text weakens these obligations by using vague language such as 'endeavor to refrain,' 'approximately two years,' 'deems adequate,' and 'try to refrain,' which makes the obligations subjective and difficult to enforce. Also, the condition of 'unless it believes it is necessary' is added which is subjective.
perturbation.law_explanation: The changes contradict the Washington Trade Secrets Act (RCW 19.108) because the Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets, but the modified contract language makes the security precautions and non-disclosure obligations subjective and discretionary, rather than clearly defined and consistently applied. The law also requires an objective standard of reasonableness which 'deems adequate' and 'unless it believes it is necessary' do not meet.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.108.010: Definitions. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.108 > Section 19.108.010 Beginning of Chapter << 19.108.010 >> 19.108.020 PDF RCW 19.108.010 Definitions. Unless the context clearly requires otherwise, the definitions set forth in this section apply throughout this chapter. (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means; (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) "Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject

perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguities and weakens obligations, contradicting the requirement to maintain secrecy as described in RCW 19.108.010. The original contract used definitive language such as 'Refrain from disclosing' and 'Take reasonable security precautions.' The revised contract uses vague terms like 'endeavor to refrain,' 'approximately,' 'deems adequate,' and 'try to refrain,' making the obligations subjective. The addition of 'unless it believes it is necessary' introduces a subjective condition that undermines the obligation. RCW 19.108.010 defines 'improper means' as including 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy.' The modified language weakens this duty, creating a contradiction because the revised contract allows for discretionary compliance, which is inconsistent with maintaining secrecy.

perturbation.original_text: (c) The parties agree to comply with all applicable international and national laws that apply to (i) any Confidential Information, or (ii) any product (or any part thereof), process or service that is the direct product of the Confidential Information, including the U.S. Export Administration Regulations, as well as end-user, end-use and destination restrictions issued by U.S. and other governments. For additional information on exporting Microsoft products, see http://www.microsoft.com/exporting/.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) The parties agree to generally consider applicable international and national laws that might apply to (i) any Confidential Information, or (ii) any product (or any part thereof), process or service that is the direct product of the Confidential Information, including the U.S. Export Administration Regulations, as well as end-user, end-use and destination restrictions issued by U.S. and other governments. Each party shall interpret and apply these laws to the best of their individual understanding.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates compliance with all applicable laws. The modification changes this to a general consideration of laws and allows each party to interpret and apply the laws based on their individual understanding. This creates ambiguity and allows for subjective interpretation, which may result in non-compliance. It also removes the mandatory nature of compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: The change conflicts with the U.S. Export Administration Regulations (EAR), specifically 15 CFR § 732.3, which dictates specific steps for determining export control requirements. By allowing parties to interpret and apply export control laws based on their 'individual understanding,' the modified text undermines the mandatory compliance and due diligence requirements of the EAR, potentially leading to unauthorized exports or violations.

perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a mandatory compliance with applicable laws with a general consideration of laws and allows parties to interpret and apply these laws based on their understanding. This contradicts the mandatory nature of laws, specifically export regulations, as suggested by the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 15 CFR 732.3, implies steps for determining export control requirements, which requires specific actions, not just general consideration or subjective interpretation. By allowing each party to interpret and apply the laws based on their individual understanding, the clause undermines the compliance requirements of the Export Administration Regulations, potentially leading to violations; therefore the contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: (f) If either Disclosing Party or the Receiving Party employs attorneys to enforce any rights arising out of or relating to this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover reasonable attorneys' fees and costs. This Agreement shall be construed and controlled by the laws of the State of Washington, and the parties further consent to exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the federal courts sitting in King County, Washington, unless no federal subject matter jurisdiction exists, in which case the parties consent to the exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the Superior of King County, Washington.
perturbation.changed_text: (f) If either Disclosing Party or the Receiving Party employs attorneys to enforce any rights arising out of or relating to this Agreement, the prevailing party may be entitled to recover attorneys' fees and costs, subject to the court's discretion. This Agreement shall be generally construed in accordance with principles of law, with the parties considering Washington law as a non-binding guideline, and the parties consent to jurisdiction and venue in a court deemed appropriate by mutual agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause provides a clear entitlement to attorneys' fees for the prevailing party and specifies Washington law and venue. The modified clause makes the recovery of fees discretionary ('may be entitled'), weakens the governing law clause by stating 'generally construed' and 'non-binding guideline,' and introduces ambiguity about the venue ('court deemed appropriate by mutual agreement').
perturbation.contradicted_law: Washington State's mandatory attorneys' fees rules in contract enforcement
perturbation.law_explanation: By making attorneys' fees discretionary and weakening the choice of law and venue provisions, the modified clause contradicts Washington's framework for awarding attorneys' fees and establishing jurisdiction. The original contract is clear about what courts have venue, but the new contract is up for debate. CR 54 outlines clear procedures and standards for awarding costs and fees to prevailing parties; the 'court's discretion' language undermines this framework. Also, the parties must consent to jurisdiction and venue in a court, while in the original, there are specific courts listed.

perturbation.justification: The modified clause introduces ambiguity regarding attorney's fees, governing law, and venue, undermining the original contract's clarity. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 generally discusses court rules and resources, the Law Explanation highlights that Washington's framework for awarding attorney's fees is being undermined. The original text is clear about what courts have venue, but the new contract is up for debate. The shift from mandatory ('shall') to discretionary ('may') for attorney's fees weakens the legal obligation, potentially conflicting with legal standards for awarding costs. The specification of Washington law as a 'non-binding guideline' also contradicts the principle of clearly defining governing law, creating uncertainty and potential legal disputes. The original contract lists specific courts, while the new contract does not, making it unclear about venue.

perturbation.original_text: (d) Receiving Party shall notify the undersigned Disclosing Party immediately upon discovery of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information or any other breach of this Agreement by Receiving Party and its employees and consultants, and will cooperate with Disclosing Party in every reasonable way to help Disclosing Party regain possession of the Confidential Information and prevent its further unauthorized use or disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: (d) Receiving Party shall endeavor to notify the undersigned Disclosing Party as soon as reasonably practical upon gaining some awareness of potential unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information or any other possible breach of this Agreement by Receiving Party and its employees and consultants, and will consider cooperating with Disclosing Party to the extent it deems feasible to help Disclosing Party regain possession of the Confidential Information and prevent its further unauthorized use or disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates immediate notification and full cooperation upon discovery of a breach. The modified text weakens this by using phrases like 'endeavor to notify as soon as reasonably practical,' 'gaining some awareness of potential unauthorized use,' 'possible breach,' and 'consider cooperating to the extent it deems feasible.' These changes introduce ambiguity and make the notification and cooperation obligations subjective.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Washington Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), RCW 19.108
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA (RCW 19.108.030) implies a duty to act reasonably to protect trade secrets once a misappropriation is known or should have been known. The modified text's vague language about notification and cooperation undermines this duty by making the response discretionary and dependent on the Receiving Party's subjective assessment of the situation, potentially delaying or preventing effective remedies for trade secret misappropriation. 'Reasonably practical' and 'to the extent it deems feasible' are too subjective. The original version requires more clear and objective behavior.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.108.030: Remedies for misappropriation—Damages. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.108 > Section 19.108.030 19.108.020 << 19.108.030 >> 19.108.040 PDF RCW 19.108.030 Remedies for misappropriation — Damages. (1) In addition to or in lieu of injunctive relief, a complainant may recover damages for the actual loss caused by misappropriation. A complainant also may recover for the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing damages for actual loss. (2) If wilful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (1). [ 1981 c 286 s 3 .]

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity that undermines the original contract's obligation to protect confidential information, potentially conflicting with the spirit of the UTSA (RCW 19.108.030) regarding remedies for misappropriation. The original text requires immediate notification and full cooperation, while the changed text uses weaker language like 'endeavor to notify as soon as reasonably practical' and 'consider cooperating to the extent it deems feasible,' which could delay or prevent effective action against misappropriation. While RCW 19.108.030 focuses on damages after misappropriation, the changes in the contract text increase the risk of misappropriation occurring or being undetected, which relates to the remedies available. The changed text makes the cooperation discretionary.
